does: This paper introduces Dynamic Cyber Ranges, an evaluation framework that augments traditional static cyber range environments with LLM-driven Defender agents capable of hardening infrastructure, monitoring for intrusions, and responding to attacks in real time. The authors demonstrate that current static cyber ranges become increasingly easy for LLM-based attackers as models improve, showing saturation effects across three infrastructure tiers from PRO Labs to military-grade ranges. To address this, they deploy both attacker and defender agents in the same environments and show that defender agents reduce attacker success rates to 0-55% across evaluated scenarios, achieving complete prevention in multiple configurations. Importantly, they find that smaller specialized models like Alias2-mini match frontier model performance on defense tasks, suggesting evaluation robustness independent of model scale.
connects: This work builds on growing research into LLM-driven autonomous agents in cybersecurity, extending beyond static benchmark evaluation frameworks like Jeopardy-style CTF competitions that have shown saturation effects. It responds to the limitation that traditional cyber range environments provide fixed, unchanging targets that attackers can eventually exploit deterministically. The paper draws on established cyber-physical evaluation methodologies where dynamic, adaptive environments better measure agent robustness, and connects to research on AI safety evaluation through adversarial testing. It also relates to work on multi-agent reinforcement learning and game-theoretic interactions, where defender-attacker dynamics create an ongoing arms race rather than static target acquisition.
enables: Dynamic Cyber Ranges establish a sustainable evaluation paradigm that maintains assessment difficulty as underlying models improve, creating a co-evolutionary testing framework where attacker and defender capabilities escalate together. This opens directions for studying emergent cybersecurity behaviors, defensive strategies, and real-time decision-making under uncertainty in realistic infrastructure contexts. The framework enables benchmarking of AI agent coordination, response speed, and strategic reasoning in adversarial settings. It could facilitate development of more sophisticated defense mechanisms and detection strategies, and provides a testbed for evaluating how well specialized smaller models perform defensive tasks versus general frontier models. Future work might explore scaling to distributed multi-agent scenarios, studying how defender and attacker agents develop novel strategies through extended interaction, and applying insights to real operational cybersecurity.
